summary: Gruber identifies three major takeaways from Apple's WWDC 2012 keynote. First, Apple has matured into a company that can develop both OS X and iOS simultaneously without one suffering for the other, unlike the Leopard delay of 2007. Second, the Retina MacBook Pro represents 'Back to the Mac' for hardware — an iOS-inspired sealed appliance that shows the future of portable Macs, much like the original MacBook Air did in 2008. Third, the entire keynote carried an unmistakable anti-Google message, with Apple systematically replacing Google services in iOS and even leveraging Facebook integration as a strategic counterweight.
key_points: Apple has overcome its earlier inability to develop OS X and iOS concurrently, with both platforms now seeing major year-over-year improvements
The Retina MacBook Pro is an iOS-inspired sealed appliance that represents the future of portable Macs, comparable to the original MacBook Air's role as industry harbinger
After even brief use, retina displays make non-retina screens look bad — the shift is perceptual and immediate
The WWDC keynote had a clear anti-Google theme, with Apple systematically removing Google from iOS and using Facebook as a strategic ally
Google's decision to compete with rather than ally with Apple on mobile was a strategic mistake
money_quotes: Apple has — dare I say finally — become a company that can walk and chew gum at the same time.
It's not so much that the retina display looks good as that, after you've used it for even just a few minutes, non-retina display MacBooks look bad.
Best display I've ever seen, period.
The new 'next-generation' MacBook Pro with Retina Display is, in short, 'Back to the Mac' for hardware.
That's what the new 15-inch MacBook Pro is: the future of portable Macs.
There was an unmistakeable 'Fuck you, Google' undertone to the whole keynote.
Even the Facebook integration feels like a 'the enemy of my enemy is my friend' alliance.
Google made a mistake by deciding to oppose rather than ally with Apple on mobile.
key_insight: WWDC 2012 revealed an Apple that had finally scaled its engineering to develop two major platforms simultaneously, was pushing hardware toward an iOS-inspired sealed future, and was waging open strategic war against Google.
